name: detr

members.name: DETRConfig
members.signature: DETRConfig(num_classes: int = 80, in_channels: int = 3, backbone_layers: tuple[int, int, int, int] = (3, 4, 6, 3), d_model: int = 256, n_head: int = 8, num_encoder_layers: int = 6, num_decoder_layers: int = 6, dim_feedforward: int = 2048, dropout: float = 0.1, num_queries: int = 100, num_bbox_layers: int = 3, bbox_hidden_dim: int = 256, score_thresh: float = 0.7)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(num_classes: int = 80, in_channels: int = 3, backbone_layers: tuple[int, int, int, int] = (3, 4, 6, 3), d_model: int = 256, n_head: int = 8, num_encoder_layers: int = 6, num_decoder_layers: int = 6, dim_feedforward: int = 2048, dropout: float = 0.1, num_queries: int = 100, num_bbox_layers: int = 3, bbox_hidden_dim: int = 256, score_thresh: float = 0.7)

members.extended:
DETR (Carion et al., ECCV 2020) reformulates object detection as a direct
set prediction problem.  A CNN backbone extracts image features, a
Transformer encoder-decoder processes them with N learned object queries,
and FFN heads predict class labels + normalised (cx, cy, w, h) boxes.
Training uses the Hungarian algorithm to match predictions to ground-truth
objects (no hand-crafted anchors, no NMS at inference).

parameters.name: dim_feedforward
parameters.description: FFN inner dimension in each transformer layer.

parameters.name: bbox_hidden_dim
parameters.description: Hidden width inside the bbox MLP.

notes:
Image → ResNet-50 (C5 feature map) → 1×1 projection (d_model)
  → flatten + 2-D sinusoidal positional encoding
→ Transformer encoder (N_enc layers, d_model, n_head)
→ Transformer decoder (N_dec layers, N_queries object queries)
→ FFN: cls head (num_classes + 1) + box head (4, sigmoid)

members.name: DETRForObjectDetection

methods.signature: forward(x: Tensor, targets: list[dict[str, Tensor]] | None = None)

parameters.name: targets
parameters.description:
Optional training targets (list of dicts with
     "boxes" (xyxy normalised) and "labels").

returns.description:
`ObjectDetectionOutput`:
`logits`    : (B, N, K+1) raw class logits.
`pred_boxes`: (B, N, 4) cxcywh normalised boxes.
`loss`      : set-prediction loss when targets provided.

methods.name: postprocess
methods.summary: Filter queries by score threshold and convert to pixel coordinates.

returns.description: List of per-image result dicts with "boxes" (xyxy pixels),

members.summary: DETR end-to-end object detector (Carion et al., ECCV 2020).
members.extended:
The first detection model to formulate object detection as a direct
**set prediction** problem, eliminating anchors and NMS.  A ResNet
backbone produces a stride-32 feature map $(B, C, H', W')$ that
is projected to $d_\mathrm{model}$ channels, flattened to a
sequence, and processed by a stack of transformer encoder layers.
A learned set of $N$ *object queries* (default 100) is then
decoded against this memory by the transformer decoder, with each
query producing one class logit vector (K+1, including
"no-object") and one box prediction $(c_x, c_y, w, h)$
normalised to $[0, 1]$.

During training, predictions and ground-truth boxes are matched by
the Hungarian algorithm under a cost combining class probability and
box L1 + GIoU, and the same combination defines the per-pair loss.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Frozen architecture spec.  Use `detr_resnet50` /
`detr_resnet101` for the paper-cited variants.

examples:
Inference returns class logits and normalised cxcywh boxes for every
query:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.detr import detr_resnet50
>>> model = detr_resnet50()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 800, 800)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (B, num_queries, K + 1)
(1, 100, 81)
>>> out.pred_boxes.shape
(1, 100, 4)
Training pass with normalised xyxy ground-truth boxes (Hungarian
matching computes the set loss):
>>> targets = [{
...     "boxes":  lucid.tensor([[0.1, 0.1, 0.5, 0.5]]),
...     "labels": lucid.tensor([3], dtype=lucid.int64),
... }]
>>> out = model(x, targets=targets)
>>> out.loss.shape
()
notes:
See Carion et al., "End-to-End Object Detection with Transformers",
ECCV 2020 ([arXiv:2005.12872](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.12872)).  The bipartite Hungarian assignment
finds the permutation $\hat{\sigma} \in \mathfrak{S}_N$ that
minimises



$$
\hat{\sigma} = \arg\min_{\sigma \in \mathfrak{S}_N}
               \sum_{i=1}^{N}
               \mathcal{L}_\mathrm{match}\bigl(y_i, \hat{y}_{\sigma(i)}\bigr),
$$

with the pair cost



$$
\mathcal{L}_\mathrm{match}(y, \hat{y}) =
    -\mathbb{1}_{c \ne \varnothing}\,\hat{p}(c)
    + \mathbb{1}_{c \ne \varnothing}\,
      \bigl(\lambda_{\mathrm{L_1}}\|b - \hat{b}\|_1
            + \lambda_\mathrm{GIoU}\,
              (1 - \mathrm{GIoU}(b, \hat{b}))\bigr).
$$

The final loss reuses the same per-pair terms, with "no-object"
matches penalised at reduced weight.  Removing NMS / anchors makes
DETR conceptually simple but training-data hungry — the paper
reports needing 500 epochs to fully converge on COCO.

attributes.name: config
attributes.description: Stored copy of the config that built this model.

attributes.name: backbone
attributes.description:
ResNet trunk through stage 5 (frozen BN), producing a stride-32
feature map.

attributes.name: query_embed
attributes.description: Learned object queries of shape `(num_queries, d_model)`.

attributes.name: transformer
attributes.description:
Reference DETR transformer (`encoder.layers.{N}` with no final
encoder norm; `decoder.layers.{N}` + a final `decoder.norm`),
with per-layer positional re-injection.

attributes.name: class_embed
attributes.description:
Per-query class head with output dim `num_classes + 1` (the
extra "no-object" slot is essential for matching).

attributes.name: bbox_embed
attributes.description:
Three-layer MLP producing per-query 4-D box predictions; the
final activation is a sigmoid so boxes lie in $[0, 1]$.

members.name: detr_resnet50
members.signature: detr_resnet50(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: DETRResNet50Weights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: DETR with ResNet-50 backbone (Carion et al., ECCV 2020).
members.extended:
Builds a `DETRForObjectDetection` with the paper-cited
ResNet-50 + transformer configuration: 100 object queries, a 6-layer
encoder + 6-layer decoder with `d_model = 256`, 8 attention heads,
and `dim_feedforward = 2048`.  The COCO config uses
`num_classes = 91`.  Reaches COCO box mAP of 42.0 (paper Table 1).
Approximately 41.5M parameters with a notable *no-anchor / no-NMS*
design.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Pretrained-weight selector.  `False` → random init; `True`
→ the `DEFAULT` tag (`DETRResNet50Weights.COCO_2017`); a
tag string → that specific checkpoint.  Mutually exclusive with
`weights` (which wins if both are given).

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit weights enum member.  Takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

returns.description:
Detector with the DETR ResNet-50 configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.detr import detr_resnet50
>>> model = detr_resnet50()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 800, 800)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(1, 100, 92)
notes:
See Carion et al., "End-to-End Object Detection with Transformers",
ECCV 2020 ([arXiv:2005.12872](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.12872)).  Pretrained weights are converted from
the original Facebook DETR `detr_resnet50` checkpoint and hosted
under `lucid-dl/detr-resnet-50`.

members.name: detr_resnet101
members.signature: detr_resnet101(pretrained: bool | str = False, weights: DETRResNet101Weights | None = None, overrides: object = {})
members.summary: DETR with ResNet-101 backbone (Carion et al., ECCV 2020).
members.extended:
Builds a `DETRForObjectDetection` with the same transformer
head as `detr_resnet50` but a deeper ResNet-101 backbone
(`[3, 4, 23, 3]` bottleneck blocks).  The COCO config uses
`num_classes = 91`.  Approximately 60.5M parameters and COCO box
mAP of 43.5 (paper Table 1, DETR-R101 row).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Pretrained-weight selector.  `False` → random init; `True`
→ the `DEFAULT` tag (`DETRResNet101Weights.COCO_2017`); a
tag string → that specific checkpoint.  Mutually exclusive with
`weights` (which wins if both are given).

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description:
Explicit weights enum member.  Takes precedence over
`pretrained`.

returns.description:
Detector with the DETR ResNet-101 configuration applied (or with
`overrides` merged on top of it).

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.vision.detr import detr_resnet101
>>> model = detr_resnet101()
>>> x = lucid.randn(1, 3, 800, 800)
>>> out = model(x)
>>> out.logits.shape
(1, 100, 92)
notes:
See Carion et al., "End-to-End Object Detection with Transformers",
ECCV 2020 ([arXiv:2005.12872](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.12872)).  Switching to ResNet-101 buys ~1.5
points of AP at the cost of ~50% more parameters in the backbone;
the transformer head is unchanged.  Pretrained weights are converted
from the original Facebook DETR `detr_resnet101` checkpoint and
hosted under `lucid-dl/detr-resnet-101`.

members.name: DETRResNet50Weights
members.extended:
Single COCO 2017 checkpoint converted from Facebook DETR's
`detr_resnet50` reference checkpoint (Carion et al., 2020; ~41.5M
params, 42.0 box mAP).

members.name: DETRResNet101Weights
members.extended:
Single COCO 2017 checkpoint converted from Facebook DETR's
`detr_resnet101` reference checkpoint (Carion et al., 2020; ~60.5M
params, 43.5 box mAP).

summary: DETR — DEtection TRansformer (Carion et al., ECCV 2020).
citation: Carion, Nicolas, et al. "End-to-End Object Detection with Transformers." Proceedings of the European Conference on Computer Vision, 2020, pp. 213–229.
theory:
DETR (DEtection TRansformer) reframes object detection as a
**direct set prediction** problem.  A CNN backbone produces a
feature map $f \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times H \times W}$, which
is flattened, projected to $d_{\mathrm{model}}$, augmented
with 2-D sinusoidal positional encodings, and fed into a standard
Transformer encoder–decoder.  The decoder additionally takes
$N$ learnable **object queries**; each query produces one
detection through FFN heads (class logits + normalised box
$(c_x, c_y, w, h)$ with a sigmoid output).

Training enforces a one-to-one assignment between predictions
and ground-truth objects via the **Hungarian algorithm**.  The
optimal permutation $\hat{\sigma}$ over $N$ slots
minimises



$$
\hat{\sigma} = \arg\min_{\sigma \in S_N}
    \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{match}}\!
        \bigl(y_i, \hat{y}_{\sigma(i)}\bigr),
$$

where the per-pair cost combines class probability and a
box-similarity term (an $\ell_1$ distance plus generalised
IoU).  Given the matched assignment, the final training loss is



$$
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{Hung}}(y, \hat{y}) =
    \sum_{i=1}^{N} \bigl[
        -\log \hat{p}_{\hat{\sigma}(i)}(c_i)
        + \mathbb{1}_{\{c_i \neq \varnothing\}}
          \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{box}}(b_i, \hat{b}_{\hat{\sigma}(i)})
    \bigr].
$$

Because each query is matched to **at most one** ground-truth
object, DETR needs neither anchor priors, nor a separate proposal
stage, nor non-maximum suppression at inference — duplicates are
suppressed by the bipartite matching during training.  The result
is the first fully end-to-end detector with a Transformer trunk;
later variants (Deformable DETR, DAB-DETR, DINO, …) build on this
set-prediction framing while accelerating convergence.
